input.background: A 1970s case-control study on cerebrovascular disease (stroke) and oral contraceptive use in young women matched cases to controls according to neighborhood, age, sex, and race. Table 18.26 displays data from this study for thrombotic stroke.
input.question: Suppose the match was broken and investigators analyzed the data as if derived from independent samples. Rearrange the information in Table 18.26 to show how it would appear in a 2-by-2 table of unmatched counts. Because the matched-pair table (Table 18.26) includes 106 pairs, your new table should total 212 individuals. Calculate the odds ratio for the data with the match broken. How does this compare to that of the (proper) matched-pair odds ratio?

output.answer:
(b) Here are the data with the match broken: 

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|} 
\hline Match broken & Case & Control & Total \\ 
\hline \multirow{3}{*}{Exposed Non-exposed Total} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
□ \\ 
46 
\end{tabular} & 7 & 53 \\ 
\hline & 60 & 99 & 159 \\ 
\hline & 106 & 106 & 212 \\ 
\hline 
\end{tabular} 

The odd ratio with the match broken is \(=(46)(99) /(7)(60)=10.8\), overestimating the more appropriate matched-pair odds ratio.

Matched pairs.description: Categories indicating whether the control in the matched pair was exposed or not exposed to oral contraceptives.

Case exposed.description: Number of cases exposed to oral contraceptives in each matched pair category.

Case not exposed.description: Number of cases not exposed to oral contraceptives in each matched pair category.

Control exposed.description: Number of controls exposed to oral contraceptives in each matched pair category.

Control not exposed.description: Number of controls not exposed to oral contraceptives in each matched pair category.

Total.description: Total number of individuals in each category and overall total in the study.
